title: What Does This Diagnosis Explain?
subtitle: A journal prompt set for processing a new health diagnosis
excerpt: A set of five journal prompts for sitting with a new health diagnosis. The prompts move from what the diagnosis explains about the past toward what you want to do with the information now.

practice.whenToUse: Use in the days or weeks after receiving a diagnosis that reframes a significant part of your past. Useful for ADHD, perimenopause, autoimmune conditions, chronic illness, or any diagnosis that has been a long time coming.

content.text: A journal prompt set for the diagnosis that explains things you had previously attributed to something else. The prompts ask what the information changes about how you understand yourself and what you want to do with it.
New to journalling as a practice? Read
What did I believe about myself before this diagnosis that I now understand differently?
Write the story you told yourself. Lazy, anxious, difficult, too sensitive, always behind. Write it without defending it or dismissing it.
What does this diagnosis explain about my past?
Name the specific moments: the jobs, the relationships, the points where you felt something was wrong but couldn't locate what it was. What looks different now?
What grief is here alongside the relief?
Both things can be true. Write what you are relieved to have named. Write also what you are mourning: the years, the misunderstandings, the things that might have gone differently.
What does this diagnosis change about how I want to treat myself going forward?
Write one specific thing: a belief about yourself you are ready to put down, a demand you are prepared to lower, a support you are willing to ask for.
What do I want to do with this information?
Not what you should do. What you actually want: more investigation, different support, a conversation with someone, time to sit with it.
